data.title: Evolent Health drops 16% after Citi downgrade on rising FY2027 Medicaid risk
data.description:
Citi Group downgraded Evolent Health (EVH) to 'neutral' from 'buy,' saying the stock's recent rally and growing FY2027 risks have made its risk-reward profile less attractive.

At the same time, the brokerage raised its price target to $6.75 from $5.50, reflecting improved near-term estimates and valuation assumptions.

Following the downgrade, the Evolent Health shares  fell more than ~16% to ~$4.68 on Thursday. 

The downgrade comes ahead of the company's second-quarter earnings, scheduled for August 6 before the market opens. Wall Street expects the company to report a loss of $0.01 per share on revenue of $599.61M.

However, Citi expects the company to deliver a solid quarter, with revenue and adjusted EBITDA likely to come in above expectations.

The firm said Evolent Health has built strong momentum heading into its second-quarter results after reaffirming its FY2026 guidance of $2.4B-$2.6B in revenue and $110M-$140M in adjusted EBITDA.

It also noted that the company's large Aetna and Highmark contracts have been successfully launched and are expected to contribute about $1B in revenue this year. Citi added that recent oncology cost trends appear to be improving.

However, the firm believes investors should remain cautious because several uncertainties remain. These include Health Insurance Exchange disenrollment, possible changes in patient acuity, and Medicaid-related headwinds in FY2027, which could reduce Medicaid membership by 5%-10%.

The brokerage also expects Medicaid work requirements to create a $10M-$15M adjusted EBITDA headwind next year.

While Citi expects a strong second quarter, it believes management is likely to maintain a cautious FY2027 outlook due to Medicaid-related risks. The firm also lowered its FY2027 adjusted EBITDA estimate by about 13.5% and said it prefers to wait for greater clarity before becoming more positive on the stock.

data.title: A & W Food Services of Canada Inc. reports Q2 results
data.description:
• A & W Food Services of Canada Inc. press release (AW:CA): Q2 Revenue of $70.8M.
  • Operating costs increased by $1.1 million (3%) to $37.2 million
  • General and administrative expenses increased by $2.0 million (18%) to $13.1 million, largely due to the biennial A&W National Convention that was held in Q2 2026
  • Income before income taxes decreased by $1.2 million (7%) to $16.0 million
  • Adjusted EBITDA(i) decreased by $1.1 million (4%) to $24.4 million and Adjusted EBITDA Margin(i) decreased 260 bps to 34.5% from 37.1% as a result of the increase in general and administrative expenses
  • Cash Dividend of $0.480 per share was declared on June 1, 2026 and paid June 30, 2026

data.title: WEX outlines 2026 revenue of $2.86B-$2.9B while prioritizing buybacks after leverage returns to 2.9x
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: WEX Inc. (WEX) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "The second quarter built on the momentum we established earlier this year. We exceeded the high end of our guidance range for both revenue and adjusted net income per diluted share." (President, CEO & Director Melissa Smith)
  • "We are progressing across each of our strategic pillars... and we are prioritizing our strong cash generation towards returning capital to shareholders in the near term." (President, CEO & Director Smith)
  • "We plan to continue to thoughtfully execute on opportunities in the back half of the year, including new pricing actions in Mobility that we expect to result in $15 million of additional revenue in 2026." (President, CEO & Director Smith)
  • "Between May and July 20, we repurchased approximately $93 million of shares, including approximately $60 million during the second quarter." (President, CEO & Director Smith)
  • "Total revenue in the quarter was $753.5 million, up 14.2% and above the top end of the guidance range we provided last quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Jagtar Narula)

Outlook

  • "In Q3, we expect to generate revenue in the range of $733 million to $753 million" and "we expect adjusted net income EPS to be between $5.45 and $5.65 per diluted share." (Chief Financial Officer Narula)
  • "For the full year, we now expect to report revenue in the range of $2.86 billion to $2.9 billion" and "we expect adjusted net income EPS to be between $19.68 and $20.08 per diluted share." (Chief Financial Officer Narula)
  • "Compared to the midpoints of the previous ranges, these represent increases of $32 million in revenue and $0.63 in EPS" and "these increases are largely driven by the outperformance in Q2, share repurchases in the quarter and higher fuel price assumptions for Q3 and the full year." (Chief Financial Officer Narula)
  • "Consistent with our past practice, we are not factoring the impact of share repurchases beyond Q2 into our guidance." (Chief Financial Officer Narula)

Financial Results

  • "Revenue for the quarter was $753.5 million" and "adjusted net income per diluted share was $5.35." (President, CEO & Director Smith)
  • "Adjusted free cash flow" was "$696 million... on a trailing 12-month basis," which "allowed us to reduce leverage to 2.9x and resume share repurchases in the quarter ahead of schedule." (President, CEO & Director Smith)
  • "Q2 adjusted operating margin increased approximately 280 basis points" and "credit losses increased from 13.5 basis points to 16 basis points, better than the range we guided you to last quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Narula)
  • "In our Benefits segment, total revenue of $206 million rose 5.6%" and "average HSA custodial cash assets grew 11.1%... and custodial investment revenue grew 11.4%." (Chief Financial Officer Narula)
  • "In Corporate Payments. Revenue of $125.1 million increased 5.8%" and "volume growth in our direct AP business reaccelerated to 20% this quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Narula)

Q&A

  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: asked about trucking “green shoots” and whether they were in the outlook; President, CEO & Director Smith: "we're not seeing more miles driven" and "we've assumed that... we're not going to see a rebound. And so to the extent we do, then that's upside to us."
  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: asked about free cash flow and buybacks/deleveraging; Chief Financial Officer Narula: "we typically generate around $600 million to $650 million of free cash flow annually" and "the vast majority of our adjusted free cash flow is going to go to share buybacks."
  • David Koning, Baird: questioned Corporate Payments volume down vs. yield up; President, CEO & Director Smith: "they moved some of the volume from the second quarter into the second half of the year" and urged investors to "look at total volume, too"; Chief Financial Officer Narula: "we would expect the rate to dip slightly... predominantly mix."
  • Tien-Tsin Huang, JPMorgan: pressed on visibility to exiting within 5% to 10% organic growth; President, CEO & Director Smith: "we... already notified customers of some pricing actions... which will bring $15 million in the second half of the year" and highlighted direct AP and embedded payments pipelines plus a "healthy" Benefits pipeline.
  • Michael Infante, Morgan Stanley: asked about competitive pressure in Mobility mid-market and fleet management take rates; President, CEO & Director Smith: "we've been keenly focused on retention" and described WEX’s "closed-loop proprietary network" and beta "AI" product.
  • Mihir Bhatia, BofA: directly challenged whether Mobility underperformance implied share loss; President, CEO & Director Smith: "we feel very confident that we continue to take market share" while also citing "demand side... still constrained by broader economic conditions."
  • Darrin Peller, Wolfe: asked about virtual card acceptance and suppression; President, CEO & Director Smith: "we do see... issues with vendor suppression" but "it is something we've factored into our guidance" and "it's not... been a big headwind."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral, with pointed framing on Mobility share and volume disconnect ("is WEX gaining share, holding share or losing share"), plus scrutiny on acceptance suppression and margin drivers. (Mihir Bhatia, BofA; Darrin Peller, Wolfe)
  • Management tone was slightly positive and operationally confident, emphasizing execution and capital return, including "we are currently prioritizing buybacks" and "we feel very confident that we continue to take market share." (President, CEO & Director Smith)
  • Versus last quarter, management language shifted from prioritizing deleveraging to explicit repurchase emphasis after hitting the leverage target early, while analyst focus shifted toward verifying share trends and reconciling Mobility growth drivers. (President, CEO & Director Smith; Chief Financial Officer Narula)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Capital allocation emphasis changed from "prioritizing debt reduction until our leverage ratio is below 3x" in Q1 to "we are currently prioritizing buybacks" in Q2 after "leverage back below 3x" and a reported "2.9x" leverage ratio. (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Melissa Smith; President, CEO & Director Smith)
  • Guidance moved higher from Q1’s full-year ranges of "$2.82 billion to $2.88 billion" revenue and "$18.95" to "$19.55" adjusted EPS to Q2’s "$2.86 billion to $2.9 billion" and "$19.68" to "$20.08," with Q2 attributing changes to "outperformance in Q2" and "higher fuel price assumptions." (Chief Financial Officer Narula)
  • Corporate Payments discussion shifted from a Q1 travel note about Middle East softness ("about $3 million a quarter") to Q2 commentary that travel customers showed "continued strength... despite... higher fuel prices and the Middle East conflict," while also highlighting OTA timing impacts to purchase volume. (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Smith; Chief Financial Officer Narula; President, CEO & Director Smith)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Lower late fee instances reduced growth slightly" in Mobility, and management said the behavior change was contemplated in guidance and expected to be offset by "planned second half pricing changes." (Chief Financial Officer Narula)
  • Corporate Payments faced quarter timing risk from "a large OTA customer" shifting volume, and management also referenced industry "vendor suppression" as a small, guided-in headwind. (President, CEO & Director Smith)
  • Mobility demand risk remained tied to macro conditions: "the demand side, which impacts our volumes, is still constrained by broader economic conditions." (Chief Financial Officer Narula)

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q2 as above-guidance on revenue and adjusted EPS and used the quarter to signal a sharper pivot toward shareholder returns after leverage reached 2.9x. The company raised Q3 and full-year guidance, tied the increase to Q2 outperformance, buybacks, and fuel assumptions, and reiterated an expectation to exit 2026 within its 5% to 10% long-term organic revenue growth range while pursuing Mobility pricing actions expected to add $15 million of 2026 revenue.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Honeywell targets 3%-4% organic sales growth and $8.20 adjusted EPS midpoint in 2026 as it raises outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Honeywell Technologies (HON) Q2 2026

Management view

  • "The second quarter marked an important milestone for Honeywell Technologies as we began our next chapter as a pure-play automation company" (Chairman & CEO Vimal Kapur). "We delivered 4% organic sales growth" and "Orders grew 16% organically" with "9% increase in ending backlog" and "segment margin" up "100 basis points" (Chairman & CEO Kapur).
  • "As a result today, we are raising our full-year outlook for organic growth, segment margin and adjusted earnings per share" (Chairman & CEO Kapur). He said the company expects "a sharp growth inflection in Process Automation and Technology" and that "we now expect to grow 4% to 6% organically in second half of the year" (Chairman & CEO Kapur).
  • "We closed the acquisition of Johnson Matthey's Catalyst Technologies business on July 17" (Chairman & CEO Kapur). He also said the company now expects "the divestitures of both Productivity Solutions and Services and Warehouse and Workflow Solutions business" to close "by early August" and that this "has reduced our 2026 revenue expectation by approximately $400 million" (Chairman & CEO Kapur).
  • "In the second quarter, Honeywell Technologies delivered strong results that surpassed our expectations" (Senior VP & CFO Mike Stepniak). "Adjusted earnings per share of $1.95 was up 10%" and "free cash flow grew considerably this quarter... to roughly $0.5 billion" (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak).

Outlook

  • "Today, we're increasing our organic sales growth outlook to 3% to 4% for the year, up from previous guidance of 2% to 3%" (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak). He added, "now expect the second half to grow 4% to 6% versus 3% to 5% previously" (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak).
  • "We now expect full year segment margin expansion of 250 to 290 basis points" and "expect Honeywell Technologies will exit the year above 22% segment margin" (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak).
  • "We now expect full year adjusted earnings per share of $8.20 at the midpoint" and "continue to expect free cash flow of roughly $2 billion in 2026... and approximately 95% conversion rate" (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak).

Financial results

  • "Sales grew 4% organically" in Q2, with "Building Automation delivered 9% organic growth" and "Industrial Automation sales were up 4%" while "Process Automation and Technology sales decreased 1% organically" (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak).
  • Profitability details included "segment margin expanded 100 basis points to 19%"; Building Automation margin "to 27.1%"; Industrial Automation margin "to 17.2%"; and Process Automation and Technology margin "contracted 180 basis points to 22.1%" (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak).
  • Cash and capital deployment included "free cash flow... to roughly $0.5 billion" and "This quarter, we deployed $1 billion of capital through roughly $800 million of dividends and roughly $200 million in high-value capital expenditures" (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak).

Q&A

  • Deane Dray, RBC: asked about Middle East collections and force majeure risk; Chairman & CEO Kapur said collection issues were "modest" and "not material" and "we don't have any risk... of any major disputes"; Senior VP & CFO Stepniak added "majority of our collection issues happened in March and April" and said revenue should be "high single digits" and orders "about 40%" in the region.
  • Deane Dray, RBC: asked about the renegotiated JM Catalyst price and integration; Chairman & CEO Kapur said the deal was done "at about 13x EBITDA with cost synergies, no sales synergies" and "we have acquired this business for commercial synergies"; Senior VP & CFO Stepniak added, "I think we got the business at the bottom."
  • Nigel Coe, Wolfe: asked what increased confidence into 2H and IA momentum; Senior VP & CFO Stepniak said "Every business printed double-digit short-cycle orders" and "the U.S. is recovering nicely" and added "there is more room... to beat even the raised guidance"; Chairman & CEO Kapur said IA is executing "quicker than we anticipated."
  • Nigel Coe, Wolfe: asked about 3Q-to-4Q margin step-up; SVP IR Mark Macaluso said improvement is "pretty broad-based" and "probably the largest increase in IA"; Senior VP & CFO Stepniak said about half the expansion is "operational" and half "structural."
  • Scott Davis, Melius: asked about M&A focus; Chairman & CEO Kapur said the "highest priority" is "strengthening our industrial automation, sensing and measurement portfolio" and also cited "8 high-growth markets" plus "tuck-in" tech.
  • Scott Davis, Melius: asked about data center opportunity; Chairman & CEO Kapur said data center was "5% of the building automation business" and cited expansion into onsite power automation and "liquid cooling" sensing.
  • Andrew Obin, BofA: asked about PA&T order drivers and derisking from Middle East; Chairman & CEO Kapur said "the biggest enabler was LNG demand" and said the company is seeing investment diversify, including "a large project in Africa" and "investments happening in Asia."
  • Andrew Obin, BofA: asked IA execution KPIs; Chairman & CEO Kapur cited delivery performance improving from "mid-40s" toward "high 80s percent" and tied momentum to new products.
  • Nicole DeBlase, Deutsche Bank: asked if BA high-single-digit growth is sustainable; Chairman & CEO Kapur said "There is no real logic" it should slow, but cited "a level of prudence" in guidance.
  • Nicole DeBlase, Deutsche Bank: asked stranded cost timing; Senior VP & CFO Stepniak said year-end stranded costs should be "about $20 million better" than prior view and "enter 2027 with about $60 million, $65 million."
  • Jeffrey Sprague, Vertical Research: asked about JM catalyst cyclicality and revenue synergies; Chairman & CEO Kapur said technology licensing end markets are "not comparable" but catalyst trends are "very convergent" and described the combined thesis as "1 plus 1 here is equal to 4."
  • Joe Ritchie, Goldman Sachs: asked if Middle East disruption is helping orders and hurting aftermarket; Chairman & CEO Kapur said activity has "resumed to normal" and pointed to digitization and logistics infrastructure investments.
  • Andy Kaplowitz, Citi: asked price vs. cost; Senior VP & CFO Stepniak said "Price was about 3.5%, 3.7%" in Q2 and "it will be around 4%" in 2H, with inflation "stubborn" in "electronics... copper... and... labor."
  • Alexander Virgo, Evercore ISI: asked about "15%" software ARR growth, repositioning costs, and catalyst margin; Chairman & CEO Kapur said ARR growth is driven by "existing offering" penetration and "launching new offerings"; Senior VP & CFO Stepniak said repositioning came from consolidating "footprint within our process business" with year estimate "around... $100 million, $110 million".
  • Christopher Snyder, Morgan Stanley: asked why Middle East looks stronger for Honeywell and M&A capacity; Chairman & CEO Kapur cited portfolio fit and a "heavily localized model" and said 2026 is focused on debt paydown, with M&A "more in the lens of 2027 onwards."
  • Andrew Buscaglia, BNP Paribas: asked LNG conversion timing and AI-driven demand; Chairman & CEO Kapur said LNG equipment deliveries are "2- to 3-year" and the company is "Totally sold out" for "3 years"; he also said Forge is "all AI-based offerings" and noted ARR moving "from about $900 million to a little over $1 billion" this year.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly positive on execution ("good news to see the guidance increasing" and "encouraging to see that LNG activity pick up") but repeatedly cautious on Middle East disruptions, collections, and regional exposure.
  • Management sentiment was positive in prepared remarks ("raising our full-year outlook") and similarly confident in Q&A, including "we feel extremely... confident in the second half" and "we're sold out LNG for the next 3 years" (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak; Chairman & CEO Kapur).
  • Compared with Q1’s emphasis on "a prudent approach" amid uncertainty, Q2 included more assertive confidence language tied to orders/backlog and guidance increases, while Middle East discussion shifted from revenue impacts and disruption to "started to normalize" (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 reframed Honeywell as "a pure-play automation company" following the Aerospace separation, whereas Q1 still included Aerospace and discussed its supply chain constraints and backlog.
  • Guidance language strengthened in Q2, with organic growth outlook raised to "3% to 4%" (from Q1’s maintained full-year outlook) and Q2 explicitly raising margin expansion and EPS outlook (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak).
  • Analyst questioning shifted from Aerospace supply chain specifics in Q1 to IA execution, PA&T inflection, stranded costs, and Middle East collections in Q2.

Risks and concerns

  • Middle East conflict and collections remained a key uncertainty: "we observed some collection issues in pockets" and the situation is "very fluid" (Chairman & CEO Kapur), while management said issues were "modest" and conditions assumed "as it is today" for planning.
  • Inflation was described as persistent: "inflation is stubborn" with continued pressure in "electronics" and "labor" (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak), with mitigation framed as pricing and productivity.
  • PA&T margin sensitivity to catalyst mix was highlighted: "margin contracted" in Q2 due to "unfavorable mix from lower catalyst volumes" (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak), while management positioned 2H volumes as "more normalized" (Chairman & CEO Kapur).

Final takeaway

Honeywell Technologies’ first quarter as a post-Aerospace, pure-play automation company centered on management’s message that Q2 performance and order momentum justified raising 2026 guidance, despite a $400 million revenue reduction from earlier divestiture timing and ongoing Middle East and inflation uncertainties. Management tied the second-half growth outlook to broad-based short-cycle strength, an expected PA&T inflection on backlog conversion and catalysts, and continued Building Automation momentum, while positioning the Johnson Matthey Catalyst Technologies acquisition as a longer-run installed-base and technology expansion with commercial synergy potential.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. stocks decline as oil reaches $100/bbl; investors assess tech results
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages slid on Thursday as tensions in the Middle East lifted oil prices to above $100 per barrel, while investors digested the latest quarterly results from giants such as Tesla (TSLA) and Alphabet (GOOGL), (GOOG).

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1.4%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -2.3%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -1.2%. 

On the earnings front, Tesla (TSLA) forecasted higher revenue, but increased capital spending weighed on free cash flow, plunging its shares 14.2% lower.

Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) fell 6.5% as the Google parent beat quarterly revenue and profit estimates, driven by an 82% jump in cloud revenue to $24.8B, though softer operating margins tempered investor sentiment.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 4 basis points to 4.71%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) was 7 basis points higher at 4.37%.

Oil prices rose above $100 per barrel after Houthi rebels reportedly struck two Saudi Arabian oil tankers in the Red Sea.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were higher at $93, while Brent (CO1:COM) was $101 per barrel.

President Donald Trump warned that the U.S. would destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant or every vessel attacked in the Strait of Hormuz. Consequently, Tehran threatened to target infrastructure and energy assets in areas where the U.S. holds interests.

On the economic side, the initial jobless claims unexpectedly sank in the past week. The Chicago Fed National Activity Index improved in June.

Bullish sentiment fell sharply while bearish views rose, according to the American Association of Individual Investors (or AAII).

“Reduced traffic [at the Strait of Hormuz], an intensification of hostilities, and rising prices are causing rates along the treasury curve to rise higher and provide one with real factors to construct a forward look on headline inflation,” said Joseph Brusuelas, principal and chief economist at RSM US.

“The June improvement in topline inflation will be mostly reversed in the July data. And remember, it’s not just oil; it's refined products like diesel and jet fuel that are rising. It’s gasoline prices that are moving higher, which is sure to feed into increasing anxiety over a serious affordability problem among the American people,” he added.

data.title: Coinbase launches three new updates for agentic AI
data.description:
Coinbase Global (COIN) on Thursday shipped three updates to span the agentic economy with specific tools for business, people, and developers.

"We believe agents are going to become the main way we access and navigate the internet across the board," the company said in a social media post. "This includes agents becoming the most active participants in the digital economy, operating on behalf of people 24/7, at a scale no human ever could."

The updates come after agentic traffic exceeded human traffic on Coinbase's (COIN) Base documentation pages for the first time last month. 

Beginning this week, Coinbase Business users will be able to accept USDC (USDC-USD) payments from AI agents. As part of the platform, businesses will also be able to manage, reconcile, and cash out agent payments in one account; earn 3.35% in rewards on idle USDC balances; and receive payments without chargeback risks.

For people, Coinbase for Agents will add real-time market views and conditional actions within guardrails set by the user.

For developers, Coinbase Developer Platform will let builders add x402 payment acceptance to any API, MCP server, or web service with three lines of code, the company said.

Coinbase (COIN) fell 2.1% in afternoon trading.

data.title: Las Vegas Sands gains as investors look past near-term headwinds
data.description:
Las Vegas Sands (LVS) went back into positive territory on Thursday after an initial post-earnings decline as analysts and investors looked past near-term headwinds.CBRE Equity Research analyst John Decree noted that an unusually low VIP hold in Macau negatively impacted EBITDA by $87M, which was only partially offset by favorable hold at Marina Bay Sands, adding $37M of EBITDA. He also noted that the World Cup held back traffic at some locations.The firm lowered FY27 EBITDA estimates on Buy-rated Las Vegas Sands (LVS) and cut its price target to $62 but still sees Las Vegas Sands (LVS) as uniquely attractive, trading at 7.5X the new FY27 EBITDA estimate. "Marina Bay Sands is arguably the most valuable casino asset in the world, with real long-term growth ahead with IR 2. Although LVS's recovery in Macau remains a show-me story, we are encouraged by the volume growth in Q2," highlighted DeCree.Macquarie also kept a bullish view on Las Vegas Sands (LVS) after the earnings release but clipped its price target. "We would view the underlying trends more constructively than the reported numbers suggest given market share gains across customer segments and strategic initiatives that we believe will bear fruit over time," updated analyst Chad Beynon.

"While near-term visibility remains imperfect, we continue to like LVS for its limited exposure to risks impacting other consumer sectors (prediction markets, gas price inputs, etc.), leverage to global wealth and experiential trends, and valuation that we believe does not fully reflect the durability of its Macau/Singapore franchises," added Beynon. Macquarie has an Outperform rating on LVS and a new price target of $62.00.Shares of Las Vegas Sands (LVS) were up 1.2% at 2:02 p.m.
